prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
*Why is the sky blue?* Well, it's not actually blue at all, but rather a light blue or a very slightly deeper shade of white that appears blue to our eyes when it is surrounded by other much darker colors when we see it from far away. This is because the small particles that make up the white clouds that cover the sky in the daytime can reflect and scatter light of all colors, but when they are scattered at certain very specific angles, they reflect and absorb more red and yellow light than they do blue and green light.

The result is that, when you look at a perfectly white cloud through an extremely dark-shaded aperture, it will look slightly blue to you, because the whiteness of the clouds is being contrasted with the much darker colors of the black hole that surrounds you. It's similar to when you look at a white object that is surrounded by darker objects; the white object will appear somewhat darker in comparison, because your eyes are comparing its color with the darker colors that surround it.

To explain this more clearly, let's imagine you have two identical pieces of white paper and you place one inside a black box with an open lid and you put a small amount of white snow on the inside of the lid. When you close the lid and look at the white paper through the darkness of the black box that is now surrounding it, the paper will look slightly more blue to you than it does when it's resting on the pure white snow. This happens because, when you're looking at something white that is surrounded by blackness, you are comparing the whiteness of that white object with the blackness that surrounds it, and your brain tells you that it looks slightly more blue than it really is.

That's why the sky appears to be blue to us humans who live on the Earth's surface; we are looking at the extremely white clouds that compose the sky from within the black shadows that are cast by the earth itself as it rotates on its axis throughout the day. The earth's shadow is vast and stretches out from the planet into space, and its colour is black, which is an absence of light. That's why, when you have a dark field with a white circle, or a white circle with a dark ring surrounding it, the white circle appears slightly darker when surrounded by the dark ring compared to when it appears on its own.

To summarize: the sky appears to be blue because we are looking at an extremely white object, the clouds, that are surrounded by the deep blackness of the earth's shadow, which makes them appear blue when seen from far away. It's similar to how when you have a white object that is surrounded by darker objects, your eyes are telling you that the white object appears slightly more blue in comparison to when the white object is on its own or surrounded by much lighter colours.
